coredata.dc:title: Construction of ratiometric fluorescence sensor and test strip with smartphone based on molecularly imprinted dual-emission quantum dots for the selective and sensitive detection of domoic acid
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Linjie
Wen, Lejuan
Chen, Yixin
Wang, Fei
Li, Caolong
coredata.dc:description: Domoic acid (DA), a highly neurotoxic metabolite produced by phytoplankton, contaminates seafood products and threats humankind. Herein, we have proposed a molecular imprinting fluorescence sensor with internal standard ratiometric mode for sensing of DA in seafood and seawater. In this study, the silicon-coated blue luminous carbon dots (B-CDs@SiO2) and CdTe acted as reference probe (430 nm) and response probe (610 nm), respectively. Subsequently, the two probes were assembled and the molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) was introduced as the recognition element to construct the core component of the sensor (B-CDs@SiO2/CdTe MIP). When DA exists, it can be specifically adsorbed by the amino-rich imprinted sites on surface of B-CDs@SiO2/CdTe MIP and further assembled into the hydrogen-bonds complex, which can lead to the decrease in the fluorescence signal of MIP at 610 nm owing to the electron transfer from CdTe to DA. However, the fluorescence signal of MIP at 430 nm is not affected because of the protection of silica layer. Based on this principle, the designed internal standard ratiometric fluorescence sensor reveals high sensitivity, excellent selectivity, and wide linear range of 0.03–1 μM with a detection limit of 18 nM. Further, the portable fluorescent test strip with smartphone has been designed for semi-quantitative sensing of DA, which has potential application prospects for field analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Domoic acid
Ratiometric fluorescence sensor
Electron transfer
Fluorescent test strip
Field analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensing of DA by B-CDs@SiO2/CdTe MIP
Fluorescent test strip with smartphone for DA sensing
Synthesis and characterization of B-CDs and CdTe QDs
Preparation and characterization of B-CDs@SiO2/CdTe MIP and NIP
The feasibility of the proposed sensor for DA analysis and detection mechanism
Quantitative analysis of DA by designed molecular imprinting ratiometric fluorescence sensor
Semi-quantitative analysis of DA via test strip with smartphone

National Key Research and Development Program
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Key Research and Development Program of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the financial support from the National Key Research and Development Program (2019YFC0312602) as well as the Jiangsu Overseas Visiting Scholar Program for University Prominent Young & Middle-aged Teachers and Presidents. The authors also appreciate the Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (KYCX22_0801).

The authors thank the financial support from the National Key Research and Development Program ( 2019YFC0312602 ) as well as the Jiangsu Overseas Visiting Scholar Program for University Prominent Young & Middle-aged Teachers and Presidents. The authors also appreciate the Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province ( KYCX22_0801 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The TEM image (A) and size distribution (B) of B-CDs. (C) The fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of B-CDs (inset is corresponding photograph under UV light or not). The TEM image (D) and size distribution (E) of CdTe. (F) The fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CdTe (inset is corresponding photograph under UV light or not). (G) FT-IR spectrum of CdTe. (H) The full XPS and (I) S 2p XPS of CdTe.
(A) SEM image and (B) elemental mappings of B-CDs@SiO2/CdTe MIP. (C) The FT-IR spectra of B-CDs@SiO2/CdTe MIP and NIP. (D) The full, (E) Si 2p, (F) Cd 3d, and (G) Te 3d XPS of B-CDs@SiO2/CdTe MIP.

(A) The possible fluorescence quenching mechanism. (B) The influence of DA on the B-CDs@SiO2/CdTe MIP, and (C) the corresponding spectra. (D) The influence of DA on B-CDs@SiO2/CdTe NIP, and (E) the corresponding spectra.

(A) The specificity and selectivity for DA analysis (DA concentration: 2 μM; interferent concentration: 1 mg L−1). (B) The influence of various levels of DA on fluorescent probe. (C) The dependence of F610/F430 on content of DA. (D) The relationship between logarithm of F610/F430 and the level of DA.

(A) The schematic illustration of test strip with smartphone for DA sensing. (B) The linear relationship logarithm of B value and DA level (the inset is the actual photo of test strip for DA with diverse levels under UV lamp).
Synthetic routes of CdTe QDs and B-CDs@SiO2 (A), and the preparation route of ratiometric fluorescence sensor and sensing mechanism for DA (B).

head.ce:title: Construction of ratiometric fluorescence sensor and test strip with smartphone based on molecularly imprinted dual-emission quantum dots for the selective and sensitive detection of domoic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Science, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing, 211198, Jiangsu, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, School of Science, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing, 211198, Jiangsu, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Cell and Biomolecule Recognition Research Center, School of Science, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing, 211198, Jiangsu, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Cell and Biomolecule Recognition Research Center

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Cell and Biomolecule Recognition Research Center, School of Science, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing, 211198, Jiangsu, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Chemistry, School of Science, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing, 211198, Jiangsu, PR China.
Corresponding author. Department of Chemistry, School of Science, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing, 211198, Jiangsu, PR China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Authors who equally contribute to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Domoic acid (DA), a highly neurotoxic metabolite produced by phytoplankton, contaminates seafood products and threats humankind. Herein, we have proposed a molecular imprinting fluorescence sensor with internal standard ratiometric mode for sensing of DA in seafood and seawater. In this study, the silicon-coated blue luminous carbon dots (B-CDs@SiO2) and CdTe acted as reference probe (430 nm) and response probe (610 nm), respectively. Subsequently, the two probes were assembled and the molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) was introduced as the recognition element to construct the core component of the sensor (B-CDs@SiO2/CdTe MIP). When DA exists, it can be specifically adsorbed by the amino-rich imprinted sites on surface of B-CDs@SiO2/CdTe MIP and further assembled into the hydrogen-bonds complex, which can lead to the decrease in the fluorescence signal of MIP at 610 nm owing to the electron transfer from CdTe to DA. However, the fluorescence signal of MIP at 430 nm is not affected because of the protection of silica layer. Based on this principle, the designed internal standard ratiometric fluorescence sensor reveals high sensitivity, excellent selectivity, and wide linear range of 0.03–1 μM with a detection limit of 18 nM. Further, the portable fluorescent test strip with smartphone has been designed for semi-quantitative sensing of DA, which has potential application prospects for field analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: Domoic acid (DA), a strong neurotoxic specie, is engendered through certain marine algae such as Pseudo-nitzschia spp., Nitzschia navis-varingica and N. bizertensis, which can enter the body via the food chain and cause serious harm to the central nervous system (Zabaglo et al., 2016; Panlilio et al., 2020). In 1987, the first poisoning incident arose from DA resulted in more than one hundred people poisoning and three deaths, which has caused a great sensation all over the world (Saeed et al., 2017). Given that, many countries and organizations have successively instituted strict standard for the level of DA in edible shellfish (20 μg/g) (Kumar et al., 2020). Thus, monitoring the level of DA in shellfish and water is of vital importance for food and environmental safety.

In the past few decades, the analysis technologies of DA have mainly relied on large-scale instruments and bioassay methods such as capillary electrophoresis (CE) (Zhang and Zhang, 2012), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Ao et al., 2018), enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) (Petropoulos et al., 2019), and mouse bioassay (MB) (Maucher and Ramsdell, 2005). However, expensive cost and cumbersome experimental procedures limit their development in practical applications. Recently, the fast analysis methods such as electrochemistry and photochemistry have widely attracted attention due to their low cost, easy operation, and portability (Dan and Wang, 2013; Nelis et al., 2021). In this respect, we have recently developed the fluorescence sensor and test paper based on molecularly imprinted carbon quantum dots for DA analysis (Wang et al., 2022). However, the designed sensor and test paper have some potential defects: (1) the output signal of fluorescence sensor based on single CDs is relatively single, and analysis results are susceptible to the interference of complex environment owing to lack of self-calibration system; (2) the fluorescent test paper based on single CDs can only reveal the variation of fluorescence brightness without abundant color change, which tremendously limits its application in quantitative sensing. In regard with these problems, exploring ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform based on dual-emission fluorescent probe for DA analysis is still highly desired.

Although the ratiometric fluorescence detection mode can well meet the analytical performance of the target species, the exceptional optical materials with good physicochemical properties are the prerequisite for the fabrication of fluorescence sensor (Arabi et al., 2021b). Surprisingly, the continuous development of quantum dots (QDs) has broadened the applications of optical sensors, especially carbon quantum dots (CDs), which has been confirmed as an ideal optical material due to their excellent optical features, water solubility, low toxicity, strong photobleaching resistance, and low cost (Alam et al., 2015; He et al., 2018). However, the currently reported CDs are mainly concentrated in the blue light-emitting region because of their low quantum yield in the long wavelength region (He et al., 2017). Fortunately, the CdTe, a widely used QD with outstanding optical characteristics, can compensate for the defects of CDs (Ensafi et al., 2017; Shahdost-Fard et al., 2021). Thus, the designed ratiometric fluorescence sensor by combining with CDs and CdTe QD can reveal a wide range of color changes, which is of great significance for rapid visual recognition. Besides, the high selectivity toward the target is another crucial indicator of the developed sensor. Molecular imprinting technology (MIT) has attracted much attention in many fields because of its function of customizing specific recognition components (Arabi et al., 2020a, 2020b; Zhang et al., 2021). As a substitute for biological antibodies, molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) by MIT not only reveals exceptional selectivity to the target, but also can compensate for the shortcomings of expensive cost, difficult acquisition, and poor stability (Ostovan et al., 2018; Arabi et al., 2021a; Rayappa et al., 2021). Therefore, the double QDs (signal probes) and MIP (recognition element) are assembled to develop an integrated ratiometric fluorescence sensor, which is highly desirable for rapid analysis of trace DA.

Herein, we have designed a double QDs-based molecular imprinting ratiometric fluorescence sensor (B-CDs@SiO2/CdTe MIP) for selective analysis of trace DA. As depicted in Scheme 1 (A), the B-CDs is coated with the silicon layer (B-CDs@SiO2) via the sol-gel method to prevent direct contact with external substances, which can be acted as a reliable reference probe. And the CdTe is prepared by condensing reflux method and served as a response probe. As displayed in Scheme 1(B), the central part (B-CDs@SiO2/CdTe MIP) of designed sensor is again prepared by a sol-gel strategy. In view of economic benefit and green concept, non-toxic and low-cost 1,3,5-pentanetrcarboxylicacid (PTA) is chosen as the pseudo template to substitute for target (DA) in this experiment (Nemoto et al., 2007; Yang et al., 2018). The prepared MIP exhibits two clear fluorescence peaks at 430 nm and 610 nm under the excitation light of 350 nm, which are attributed to the reference probe and response probe, respectively. After the introduction of DA, the fluorescence signal at 610 nm gradually disappears with the increase of DA level, while the one at 430 nm is almost unchanged. Meanwhile, it is accompanied by continuous fluorescence color changes from orange yellow to blue, which can be read out by the color recognition application of the smartphone (Wu and Ozcan, 2018; Hunt et al., 2021a, 2021b; Liu et al., 2021; Gordon et al., 2022; Skolrood et al., 2022). Thus, the proposed test strip with smartphone can achieve semi-quantitative sensing of DA. More importantly, this attractive ratiometric fluorescence sensor is reliable and sensitive for sensing of DA in actual samples.
The reagents and instruments used are listed in Supporting Information (SI).

B-CDs was prepared by using citric acid as the carbon source and urea as the nitrogen and carbon source through a classical hydrothermal method. Briefly, 1.5 mmol urea and 1 mmol citric acid monohydrate were dissolved in 20 mL deionized water by ultrasound, and then quickly transferred into an autoclave and heated for 4 h at 180 °C. After cooling down to the ambient temperature, the supernatant was passed through 0.22 μm membranes (polyethersulfone, PES) to remove impurities, and then transferred to a dialysis bag for 24 h. Finally, the obtained product was dispersed into 100 mL deionized water, followed by storing in the refrigerator for further use.

The B-CDs@SiO2 was prepared by a sol-gel method, and the detailed preparation process was as follows. Firstly, 1 mL B-CDs solution and 200 μL NH3(aq) were sequentially added to 50 mL water-ethanol solution system (2:1, V/V) and stirred for 30 min. Subsequently, 100 μL TEOS was introduced drop by drop and continued to stir overnight under the ambient temperature. Finally, the obtained B-CDs@SiO2 was collected by centrifugation, and washed with ultrapure water and ethanol for several times, followed by drying at 60 °C for further use.

The synthesis strategy of CdTe QDs referred to the reported literature with slight modifications (Shi et al., 2019). Specifically, 110 mL ultrapure water and 0.3 mL 3-MPA were sequentially introduced into a 250 mL three-necked flask and stirred for 5 min. Then, 0.44 g CdCl2 was added into the above solution and continued to stir for 30 min. Afterward, the pH value of mixture was regulated to approximately 11 with NaOH solution (1 M), and then 0.1 g NaBH4 was introduced. Subsequently, 10 mL Na2TeO3 aqueous solution (0.1 M) was slowly dripped to the reaction system and refluxed at 100 °C for 12 h. After cooling down to ambient temperature, the reaction product was purified through a precipitation-centrifugation method with ethanol for three times. Eventually, the collected solid was suspended in 20 mL deionized water, followed by storing in the refrigerator for further use.

The one-pot sol-gel strategy was again applied to manufacture B-CDs@SiO2/CdTe MIP in this research. In detail, 0.8 mL prepared CdTe QDs and 0.1 mL APTES were added into 50 mL ethanol and stirred for 6 h. Afterward, 30 mg B-CDs@SiO2 powder, 20.4 mg PTA, and 0.1 mL TEOS were successively introduced into the above mixed solution.

Subsequently, the mixture was stirred for another 30 min, and then 0.2 mL ammonia was added into the above solution and continued to stir for 24 h. After that, the synthesized product was obtained through centrifugation and eluted with methanol and ultrapure water through the centrifugal-ultrasonic method to remove the dummy template and impurities. Eventually, the product was dried at 60 °C for later use. In addition, the preparation process of B-CDs@SiO2/CdTe NIP was same but without PTA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of DA by B-CDs@SiO2/CdTe MIP
ce:section.ce:para: All fluorescence tests were performed on Shimadzu RF-5301PC in this research. Specifically, 0.1 mL DA with different concentration were added into 0.07 mL probe solution (1 mg mL−1), respectively. Subsequently, these above mixtures were incubated at 25 °C for 10 min. Afterward, 2.83 mL ultrapure water was introduced into above mixtures and measured. The excitation signal was 350 nm, and emission signal at 430 nm and 610 nm were measured and recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent test strip with smartphone for DA sensing
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared fluorescence test strip combined with smartphone can realize semi-quantitative analysis of DA. In detail, the ordinary filter papers were cut into small round pieces with a diameter of 1.8 cm. Afterward, 0.07 mL probe solution and 0.1 mL DA with different concentration were dipped evenly onto small round pieces in turn, followed by the incubation for 10 min in the dark. Finally, the color response of test strip was presented under a 365 nm UV lamp, and the Blue (B) color channel value of test strip was recorded by using the free android application called “Color Grab”.
In addition, the DA in actual samples were detected by using the designed sensor via spike and recovery test. The related contents are displayed in Supporting Information (SI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of B-CDs and CdTe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The B-CDs was prepared by a classical hydrothermal method. As depicted in Fig. 1 A and Fig. 1B, the well-dispersed B-CDs reveals spherical shape with an average particle size of about 1.5 nm. The distinct absorption signal of B-CDs at 340 nm is ascribed to n-π* transition of CO and other surface moieties (Fig. S1) (Huang et al., 2020). It is clearly observed from Fig. 1C that 350 nm and 440 nm are optimal excitation and emission signals, respectively. Visibly, B-CDs aqueous dispersion reveals strong fluorescence under UV light (the inset in Fig. 1C). Besides, the emission peak gradually diminishes as excitation signal moves away from 350 nm. However, the position of the emission signal is hardly shifted (Fig. S2). Additionally, the fluorescence quantum yield of B-CDs was calculated to be 14.4% by means of the integrated FL intensity (The details are shown in Support Information). The B-CDs@SiO2, acted as a reliable reference probe, was prepared by coating B-CDs with SiO2 matrices to prevent direct contact with external substances (Liu et al., 2019). As depicted in Fig. S3, the synthetized B-CDs@SiO2 reveals homogeneous sphere with a diameter of about 200 nm. Compared with B-CDs, the optimal excitation and emission signals of B-CDs@SiO2 are marginally shifted, which may be arose from the SiO2 matrices. However, the B-CDs@SiO2 solution still exhibits distinct blue luminescence under UV light (Fig. S4), and the emission signal intensity of B-CDs@SiO2 also correlates with the excitation signal utilized. As displayed in Fig. S5, emission signal gradually diminishes and even becomes unrecognizable as excitation signal moves away from optimal value.

The CdTe was synthesized via condensing reflux method and served as a response probe. As shown in Fig. 1D and E, the CdTe presents the tendency of agglomeration, while the single point particles are still clearly visible with the diameter of around 3.3 nm. According to Fig. 1F, the 300 nm and 608 nm are the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of CdTe, respectively. Obviously, the CdTe aqueous solution exhibits bright orange yellow luminescence under UV lamp (inset in Fig. 1F). Similar to the B-CDs, the emission signal of the CdTe gradually diminishes as excitation signal moves away from optimum wavelength (Fig. S6). The surface groups of MPA-capped CdTe were measured by FT-IR. The peaks at 1552 and 2920 cm−1 are assigned to carboxylate anion vibration and alkyl chains stretching vibration of MPA, respectively. The FT-IR signal at around 3410 cm−1 originates from O–H vibration, and band at 1395 cm−1 should be C–O symmetric stretching vibration. The inexistence of S–H peak at 2550 cm−1 suggests that the MPA is linked to Cd2+ on the CdTe surface through sulfur atom on sulfhydryl group (Fig. 1G) (Huang et al., 2016). The element compositions and contents, chemical states, molecular structures, and chemical bonds of MPA-capped CdTe were measured by XPS. The full XPS result (Fig. 1H) confirms the existence of C, O, S, Cd, and Te in the prepared MPA-capped CdTe, and the high-resolution spectrum of S 2p (Fig. 1I) can be presented two peaks of S 2p1/2 and S 2p3/2, which are related to CdS and thiol, respectively (Gong et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2019). The above results strongly demonstrate the successful preparation of MPA-capped CdTe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of B-CDs@SiO2/CdTe MIP and NIP
ce:section.ce:para: The B-CDs@SiO2/CdTe MIP was synthesized by sol-gel method and characterized by several means. As we all known that the imprinted layer thickness of MIP has a tremendous impact on the recognition performance to DA. The MIPs prepared with different ratios of functional monomer (APTES) and cross-linking agent (TESO) were investigated, and the relevant performance results are shown in Table S1. The optimal performance sensor uses MIP made from 0.1 mL APTES and 0.1 mL TEOS, which is employed in the following experiments. The micromorphology of B-CDs@SiO2/CdTe MIP was measured by SEM. The SEM image exhibits that the prepared CdTe MIP with particle morphology is blended with B-CDs@SiO2, and B-CDs@SiO2 is partially coated by CdTe MIP (Fig. 2 A). And the corresponding elemental mappings demonstrate that the elements of C, N, O, Si, S, Cd, and Te are uniformly distributed in synthesized material (Fig. 2B). Moreover, the B-CDs@SiO2/CdTe NIP demonstrates analogous shape and element mappings to MIP (Fig. S7). As for FT-IR analysis (Fig. 2C), the Si–O bond contributes to the signals at 455 and 780 cm−1, and characteristic signal at 1050 cm−1 originates from the typical Si–O–Si stretching, which demonstrates the presence of SiO2 matrices. Moreover, the stretching vibration bands of N–H about 3430 and 1555 cm−1 and C–H stretching band at 2930 cm−1 indicate the presence of APTES in the prepared imprinted polymers (Jalili et al., 2020). The full range XPS spectrum confirms the existence of C, O, Si, Cd, and Te elements (Fig. 2D), and in Si 2p XPS (Fig. 2E), the peaks at 102.1 and 102.8 eV should be attributed to Si–C and Si–O, respectively. The Cd 3d spectrum evidently supplies two peaks centered at 404.4 and 411.2 eV, ascribed to the Cd 3d5/2 and Cd 3d3/2 species, respectively (Fig. 2F). Besides, Fig. 2G reveals the Te 3d peaks at 572.0 and 582.0 eV, which are attributed to the Te 3d5/2 and Te 3d3/2, respectively (Liu et al., 2019). All the above results prove that the MIP and NIP are successfully prepared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The feasibility of the proposed sensor for DA analysis and detection mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: The recognition process for DA by the B-CDs@SiO2/CdTe MIP was clearly displayed in Scheme 1(B). The B-CDs@SiO2/CdTe MIP were synthesized by using sol-gel method with PTA as the pseudo template molecule in this experiment. The pseudo template and monomer pre-assembled through the strong non-covalent interaction between APTES-modified CdTe QDs and PTA. Then B-CDs@SiO2/CdTe MIP with specific imprinted cavities were synthesized through the copolymerization of PTA (pseudo template), APTES (functional monomer), TEOS (cross-linker), NH3·H2O (initiator), and B-CDs@SiO2 (internal standard QD). Since the hydrogen bond could serve as a connecting channel between the pseudo template and the functional monomer, the amino groups of APTES can interact with the functional group of PTA such as the carboxyl group through hydrogen bonding. After elution, molecularly imprinted cavities with custom sizes and sites are formed. The DA with the optimal size and shape complementarity can enter the imprinted cavity and bind with the functional group on the binding site in the cavity, which can affect the fluorescence intensity of MIP. The possible sensing mechanism is depicted in Fig. 3 A. The DA can interact with electron-rich amino groups provided by APTES on the surface of imprinted layer via the hydrogen bonding, and the electron transfer process between CdTe and DA leads to fluorescence quenching (Xu et al., 2013; Wei et al., 2014). In this research, the amino-rich imprinted sites on surface of B-CDs@SiO2/CdTe MIP can specifically adsorb DA and further assemble into the hydrogen-bonds complex (Fig. 3B), which can lead to the decrease in the fluorescence signal of MIP at 610 nm owing to the electron transfer from CdTe to DA (Fig. 3C). However, very small amount of DA adheres to the surface of NIP via nonspecific adhesion due to the lack of specific molecularly imprinted cavities (Fig. 3D), which results in a slight decrease in fluorescence intensity of NIP at 610 nm (Fig. 3E). Fascinatingly, the introduced DA reveals negligible impact on the fluorescence signal of B-CDs (430 nm) because of the protection of silica layer, which is beneficial for the construction of fluorescence sensor with internal standard ratiometric mode. Thus, the designed B-CDs@SiO2/CdTe MIP-based ratiometric fluorescence sensor with internal standard mode can be well applied for analysis of DA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative analysis of DA by designed molecular imprinting ratiometric fluorescence sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental parameters including dosage of fluorescent probe, incubation time, and the pH of sensing system were optimized before sample analysis. The fluorescence intensities of the designed sensor at 610 nm with or without DA were recorded to investigate the optimal dosage of fluorescent probe. As displayed in Fig. S8, the fluorescence quenching efficiency ((F(610)0-F610)/F(610)0) reveals the maximum value when the amount of fluorescent probe used is 70 μL. Therefore, 70 μL of the fluorescent probe is regarded as the optimal dosage in the sensing system considering the analysis performance of sensor. Besides, the optimal incubation time of fluorescent probe and DA was explored to obtain better analytical performance of the designed sensor. As illustrated in Fig. S9, the value of F610/F430 decreases sharply within 5 min and turns to be gradually saturated at around 10 min. Therefore, the incubation time is set to 10 min considering the accuracy of the analysis results. The pH value of the sensing system is also a crucial factor for the performance of MIP sensor. As demonstrated by Fig. S10, the fluorescence intensity ratios of the designed sensor between 610 nm and 430 nm with or without DA are all affected by pH. Obviously, the (F610/F430)0 emerges the maximum value and the F610/F430 reveals the minimum value when the pH of the sensing system is 7. Therefore, the calculated value of (F610/F430)/(F610/F430)0 exhibits the minimum under neutral condition. These results indicate that DA has the maximum response to the designed sensor when the pH of the sensing system is 7. Hence, the use of near-neutral ultrapure water in the sensing system is conducive to enhancing the sensitivity and linear range of the sensor. Besides, the influence of ionic strength on sensing response of DA was investigated. As demonstrated by Fig. S11, the effect of ionic strength on sensor itself and the signal response of the sensor toward DA can be ignored.

The specificity and selectivity are key indicators for assessing practicability of developed sensor. Therefore, some possible co-existence interferents, including Gln, Glr, Glu, K+, Zn2+, Al3+, SO4 2−, Cl−, Ac−, NO3 −, CO3 2−, and PO4 3− were evaluated in the absence and presence of target DA. As depicted in Fig. 4 A, these interferents alone except for Gln, Glr, and Glu reveal negligible effects on the F610/F430 of designed fluorescence sensor in comparison with the blank control group. The molecular structures of Gln, Glr, and Glu are similar to DA, which brings challenges to the recognition function of the sensor. However, they all have an extremely weak impact on the sensing system in comparison with DA thanks to the strong recognition ability provided by MIPs. Surprisingly, the sensing system exhibits superior anti-interference ability when DA coexists with each interferent separately. Based on the above results, the developed sensor is satisfactory in terms of selectivity and specificity.

To check the reliability of our analysis strategy, the spiked actual samples were detected. The samples pretreatment and detection processes were described in detail in supporting information. Different concentrations of DA are spiked in actual samples for recovery experiment and the data (Table.S3) demonstrate that designed ratiometric fluorescence sensor has excellent recoveries (93%–108%) for DA sensing in spiked samples. Thus, the proposed sensor has great promising in practical application owing to its outstanding reliability in the analysis of actual samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Semi-quantitative analysis of DA via test strip with smartphone
ce:section.ce:para: To further make analysis of DA more portable, we have developed the portable fluorescent test strip with smartphone for semi-quantitative analysis of DA. As demonstrated by Fig. 5 A, the fluorescent probe solution is uniformly coated on the surface of the small round piece cut from ordinary filter paper to prepare test strip. After various levels of DA are added and incubated for 10 min, the fluorescent test strip reveals the color response from orange yellow to blue under 365 nm UV lamp (The inset of Fig. 5B). Subsequently, the photograph of the test strip under UV lamp is taken through the camera function of smartphone, and the RGB values of test strips in the photograph are recorded by using the free application called “Color Grab”. Surprisingly, the logarithm of Blue (B) color channel value is linearly related to DA levels in the range of 0.05–10 μM (Fig. 5B), and the linear equation can be fitted as ln(B) = −0.051[DA] + 5.380 (R 2 = 0.997). However, it should be mentioned that the developed test strip with smartphone is a little difficult to distinguish samples with slightly difference in DA level, because (1) The loading of the fluorescent probe on the test strip is not completely uniform, resulting in differences in the overall color response on the test strip; (2) The color recognition application has certain difficulties in identifying subtle color differences. Therefore, the designed fluorescent test strip with smartphone can only be used as an auxiliary means of fluorescence instrument analysis for the semi-quantitative analysis of DA. How to solve these problems and make fluorescent test strip with smartphone play a greater role in practical applications still requires our continuous efforts.

In short, we have fabricated an internal standard ratiometric fluorescence sensor for rapid sensing of DA. The sensor reveals excellent selectivity, outstanding sensitivity, and good reliability in complex matrices because of the contribution of dual quantum dots fluorescent probe and MIT. More importantly, the portable fluorescent test strip with smartphone has been designed for on-site semi-quantitative analysis of DA. Our strategy and test strip are considered to have potential application prospects in the analysis of trace marine toxins especially DA owing to its outstanding performance and portable operation.

Linjie Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data Curation, Writing–original draft. Lejuan Wen: Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Writing–Review & Editing. Yixin Chen: Formal analysis, Validation. Fei Wang: Visualization, Supervision, Resources. Caolong Li: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the financial support from the National Key Research and Development Program (2019YFC0312602) as well as the Jiangsu Overseas Visiting Scholar Program for University Prominent Young & Middle-aged Teachers and Presidents. The authors also appreciate the Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (KYCX22_0801).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.chemosphere.2022.135405.
